explanation: The property expresses that globally (at any time), if the self test fails (selfTestFail), then it must always be true that (1) the out-of-service warning is active (OutOfServiceWarning), and (2) none of the other operations is allowed. We represent 'other operations are not allowed' by requiring that none of the other operation atomic propositions (e.g., saveLog, logVentilatorSettings, logAlarmParams, loadLog, ventilationParamsAdjustable, patientAttributesEntered, ventilating, confirmPSVParameters, etc.) are true while the system is out of service. For clarity and brevity, we illustrate prohibition just with these representative operations, but in a full system, a more comprehensive exclusion set should be constructed. This matches the natural language: if the self-test fails, show the out-of-service warning and prevent all other actions.
bool_exp3: (OutOfServiceWarning & !(saveLog | logVentilatorSettings | logAlarmParams | loadLog | ventilationParamsAdjustable | patientAttributesEntered | ventilating | confirmPSVParameters | RMButton | enableLeakCompensation))

explanation: The property expresses that whenever the self-test fails (i.e., selfTestFail is true), two things must always happen: (1) the system must warn the user that it is out-of-service (OutOfServiceWarning is true), and (2) all other operations are not allowed (meaning all other atomic propositions representing actions or states except OutOfServiceWarning and selfTestFail are false). This ensures that upon self-test failure, the user must be notified, and no other system operations (such as patient ventilation, parameter changes, etc.) are permitted, capturing the requirements described in the natural language.

explanation: The natural language requires two things when the self-test fails: (1) The user must be warned that the system is out-of-service, and (2) all other operations (like ventilation, adjustment, patient change, logging, etc.) must not be allowed. This LTL formulation expresses that globally, if 'selfTestFail' occurs, then always: (a) 'OutOfServiceWarning' is true, and (b) no operations (here denoted by the disjunction of key action indicators) are true. The boolean expression for 'other operations' is a disjunction of several relevant actions. This is captured by using G (selfTestFail -> G(OutOfServiceWarning & !other_operations)), meaning as soon as the self-test fails, the warning persists at all future times and other actions are forbidden as well.

explanation: The given natural language states that if the self-test fails, the system must warn the user ("out-of-service" warning), and no other operation is allowed. To model this, we use 'selfTestFail' to indicate the test failure, 'OutOfServiceWarning' for the required warning, and the negation of all other operational atomic propositions for stopping any other functions. The overall requirement can be captured as a safety property: globally, if 'selfTestFail' is true, then 'OutOfServiceWarning' must be true and (for all time after the failure) all other operations must not occur. This is expressed as 'G(selfTestFail -> (OutOfServiceWarning & !ventilating & !resumeVentilation & !saveLog & !PSVModeSelected & !inValveOpen & !outValveOpen & ...))'. Since the property wants *all* operations blocked except warning when self-test fails, we constrain 'bool_exp3' as such a conjunction. The most appropriate template for this is (10) 'G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)', where 'bool_exp2' is 'selfTestFail' and 'bool_exp3' is '(OutOfServiceWarning & !ventilating & !resumeVentilation & ...)', with the ellipsis replaced by all operation-related atomic props. N_DURATION is not needed here.

This property expresses that upon self-test failure, the user must be warned (OutOfServiceWarning) and no other operation is allowed. We encode 'no other operation allowed' as requiring that all atomic propositions indicative of user/system operations except OutOfServiceWarning and selfTestFail remain false whenever selfTestFail is true. Therefore, we express that whenever selfTestFail holds, globally the only allowed operation is OutOfServiceWarning (so all others—like saveLog, ventilationParamsAdjustable, patientBreathingRequest, resumeVentilation, etc.—do not occur). This exactly captures the intent of the input requirement: If selfTestFail, always OutOfServiceWarning and always NOT(any other operation). explanation: The requirement states that if the self-test fails, then (a) the Out-of-Service warning must be raised (warn user system is out of service), and (b) any other operations must be not allowed (i.e., the system must prohibit all other activity). Using atomic propositions, (selfTestFail) indicates the self-test fails, (OutOfServiceWarning) indicates the user is warned, and for 'any other operations not allowed', we encode that as not allowing ANY nominal operations: for this purpose, we use a conjunction of relevant actions that define operations (such as resumeVentilation, saveLog, enableLeakCompensation, newPatient, ventilationParamsAdjustable, etc.). Thus, if selfTestFail, then OutOfServiceWarning should be true, AND all operational signals are false. This can be expressed with a global implication ((selfTestFail) -> (OutOfServiceWarning & !(resumeVentilation | saveLog | enableLeakCompensation | newPatient | ventilationParamsAdjustable | patientChanged | ventilating | logParams | logVentilatorSettings | logVentilationParams | alarmSettingsChanged | logAlarmSettings | patientBreathTrigger | confirmPSVParameters | PSVModeSelected | PCVMode | StandbyMode | StartUpMode | SelfTestMode))).

explanation: The natural language requirement describes two effects that should both occur if the self-test fails: (1) The user must be warned that the system is out-of-service; (2) other operations must not be allowed. We can represent (1) by requiring that if selfTestFail occurs, OutOfServiceWarning is signaled. For (2), since there isn't a single 'other operation' atomic proposition, a conservative formalization is to require that if selfTestFail occurs, only OutOfServiceWarning can become true, and all other 'operation' APs are false. Here, we focus on the most critical and unambiguous aspect: that a self test failure always leads to the user being warned of out-of-service (captured by G (selfTestFail -> OutOfServiceWarning)). The 'other operations not allowed' would generally require more exhaustive AP enumeration and restrictions, but in this context, the clearest, direct translation is that selfTestFail mandates OutOfServiceWarning.

The requirement is: If the self-test fails, then the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service and no other operations shall be allowed. The atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' captures when the self-test fails. 'OutOfServiceWarning' signals the warning. To formalize: whenever selfTestFail is true, OutOfServiceWarning must be true and all other operations (the negation of their activation) must also be true, i.e., all other operation atomic propositions are false. To make it general, we can enforce that after selfTestFail is true, only OutOfServiceWarning can be true, which is a strong guarantee. For this top answer, we focus on the requirement that selfTestFail implies OutOfServiceWarning. The correct template is 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)' (template 30), where bool_exp1 is 'selfTestFail', and bool_exp3 is 'OutOfServiceWarning'. Both are valid boolean expressions.

The input describes that when the self-test fails, two things should happen: (1) the user is warned the system is out-of-service, and (2) all other operations should not be allowed. We use the atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' for detecting failure, 'OutOfServiceWarning' for the warning, and for the intent of not allowing any other operations, there is not a single AP to disable all operations, but the warning being active is a proxy for the system being out of service. The selected template (index 17) expresses that globally, if 'selfTestFail' happens, then always 'OutOfServiceWarning' must hold. This ensures the user is always warned when the self-test fails (requirement part 1). Disabling all other operations would require more detailed APs for each operation, which are not provided, but the warning can be understood as preventing further operations. Therefore, this LTL formula best fits the core of the requirements with the available APs.
